required.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of perseverance

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] The Story of Ruby Bridges
suggested.author: Robert Coles

suggested.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Salt in His Shoes
suggested.author: Deloris Jordan and Roslyn M. Jordan

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Biographies of inventors and explorers

competence_markers.description: After a mistake or failure, the child's default response trends toward trying again rather than giving up — not every time, but as a pattern
The child can articulate what they learned from something that went wrong, in their own words
The child does not consistently equate a failed outcome with personal failure — they can separate "it didn't work" from "I'm bad at this"
The child can identify effort as valuable even when the outcome was not what they wanted
The child engages with stories of perseverance and can discuss what the characters did and why
The child is beginning to ask "What can I learn from this?" on their own, without adult prompting
The child can name the difference between what they can control (effort, attitude, trying again) and what they cannot control (outcomes, other people, circumstances)
During the weekly review, the child can identify a hard thing they faced and describe their response to it

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about something that did not work the first time you tried it.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What did you do? Did you try again? What was different the second time?
Is a mistake a bad thing? What can you learn from a mistake?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about someone from a story who kept trying even when things were hard.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened to them? Why did they keep going? What would have happened if they stopped?

narration_prompts.prompt: Have you ever worked really hard at something and it still did not turn out the way you wanted?
narration_prompts.detail: How did that feel? Does that mean the hard work was wasted?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between what you can control and what you cannot control?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you control how hard you try? Can you control whether you win?

narration_prompts.prompt: What was the hardest thing you did this week?
narration_prompts.detail: What did you learn from it?

cross_connections.description: Reframing requires emotional awareness. The child who can name what they are feeling (IC3.1) can then examine the story they are telling themselves about it (IC3.3). These two capacities develop together — emotional regulation makes reframing possible, and reframing strengthens emotional regulation.

The perseverance trained here is the internal counterpart to the behavioral discipline of IC1.1. The child who can finish a task they do not want to finish (IC1.1) and interpret setbacks as information rather than defeat (IC3.3) has both the behavioral and cognitive foundations for resilience.

History provides the stories of perseverance that IC3.3 uses as formation material. The people who built civilizations, made discoveries, and changed the world are almost always people who failed repeatedly and kept going.

IC3.3 provides the cognitive framework for what IC3.4 does physically and experientially. The child learns to think about difficulty differently (IC3.3) while also experiencing small, controlled difficulties directly (IC3.4). Each reinforces the other.
The "try again" orientation is character in action. The child who perseveres despite setbacks is exercising the virtues of courage and persistence that IC1 develops across all its sub-domains.

content.why_this_matters:
Something is going to go wrong today. Maybe not a big thing. Maybe you will drop something, or your tower of blocks will fall, or you will try to do something and it will not work the first time. Maybe someone will say something that makes you feel bad. Maybe you will work very hard on something and it still will not turn out the way you wanted.

This is not a problem. This is life. Things go wrong for everyone, every day — for children and for adults. The question is not whether hard things will happen. The question is: **what will you do when they happen?**

Some people, when something goes wrong, stop trying. They say, "I can't do it." They say, "I'm bad at this." They say, "It's not fair." And then they sit down and do nothing. Other people, when the same thing goes wrong, look at what happened, figure out what they can learn from it, and try again — maybe a different way this time.

The difference between those two kinds of people is not luck. It is not that the second group is smarter or stronger. It is that the second group has learned a way of thinking. They have learned that **a mistake is not the end of the story — it is information about what to do next.**

This packet teaches that way of thinking. It is one of the most important things you will ever learn, because it determines whether hard things in your life make you weaker or make you stronger.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The "try again" orientation** — the automatic response to a first failure is to attempt again, not to quit
2. **Mistakes as information** — the habit of asking "what did this teach me?" rather than "why did I fail?"
3. **Separating effort from outcome** — understanding that you can try your hardest and still not get the result, and that this does not mean the effort was wasted
4. **Perseverance through stories** — real accounts of people who failed, learned, and continued, forming a mental library of what persistence looks like
5. **The beginnings of internal locus of control** — the earliest understanding that what you do about a problem matters more than the problem itself
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Happens When Something Goes Wrong

When something goes wrong — you drop the glass, you fall off the bike, you get the answer wrong, you lose the game — your brain does something very fast. Faster than you can think about it, your brain tells you a story about what just happened.

Sometimes the story is: "That was hard. I'll try again."

Sometimes the story is: "I'm terrible at this. I should stop."

Both stories happen automatically. The first story leads to trying again, learning, and eventually getting better. The second story leads to giving up. **The story your brain tells you matters more than what actually happened.**

Here is the important part: **you can change the story.** Not the event — the event already happened. The glass is broken, the bike tipped over, the answer was wrong. You cannot undo that. But you can change what you tell yourself about it. And what you tell yourself about it determines what happens next.

This is called **reframing**. It means looking at the same thing from a different angle — the way you can look at a building from the front and it looks one way, and then walk around to the side and it looks completely different. The building did not change. Your position changed. And from the new position, you can see things you could not see before.

**Example:**
- What happened: You tried to build a tall tower and it fell down.
- Story 1: "I can't build towers. I'm bad at this." (This story makes you stop.)
- Story 2: "It fell because the bottom was too narrow. I need a wider base." (This story makes you try again with new information.)

Both stories are about the same tower falling down. But Story 2 turns the failure into a lesson. The tower falling was not a disaster — it was data. It told you something useful. Now you know something you did not know before.

### Part 2: Mistakes Are Information

A mistake is not a bad thing that happened to you. A mistake is **information about what does not work.**

Thomas Edison, when he was trying to invent a working lightbulb, tested thousands of materials before finding one that worked. When someone asked him about all those failures, he said he had not failed — he had found thousands of ways that did not work. Each "failure" eliminated a possibility and brought him closer to the answer.

This is not just a nice story. It is actually how learning works. Your brain learns more from getting something wrong and figuring out why than from getting it right the first time. When you get it right immediately, your brain does not have to work very hard. When you get it wrong, your brain has to figure out what happened, what was different from what you expected, and what to change. That figuring-out process is where real learning happens.

**The question to ask after a mistake is not "Why did I fail?"**
**The question is: "What did this teach me?"**

- You misspelled a word. What did that teach you? That the word does not follow the pattern you expected. Now you know.
- You missed the ball. What did that teach you? That you were watching the wrong spot. Now you know where to look.
- You said something that hurt someone's feelings. What did that teach you? That those words land differently than you intended. Now you know.

Every mistake is a teacher — but only if you pay attention to the lesson.

### Part 3: Trying Hard and Not Getting the Result

This is a harder idea, and it is one of the most important ones.

Sometimes you try your very best — you work as hard as you can, you pay attention, you do everything right — and it still does not work out. You lose the race even though you trained. You do not get picked even though you practiced. The plant you cared for dies. The project does not turn out the way you imagined.

This feels terrible. And here is where many people make a dangerous mistake. They think: "I tried hard and it didn't work, so trying hard must not matter."

**That is wrong.** Trying hard always matters. But trying hard does not guarantee the result. These are two different things:

- **Effort** is what you control. You can always choose to try, to work, to practice, to prepare.
- **Outcome** is what happens. Outcomes depend on effort, but also on things you cannot control — other people's choices, weather, timing, luck, difficulty.

The person who understands this difference is free. They can try their hardest without being destroyed when the result does not come. They can lose a game and still know that their preparation was good. They can fail a test and still know that their studying was real. The effort was not wasted just because the outcome was not what they wanted.

**What effort always produces — even without the desired outcome:**
- You got better. Even if you lost, you are stronger, faster, or more skilled than you were before you tried.
- You learned something. The attempt taught you things that not attempting could never teach.
- You proved something about yourself. You are the kind of person who tries. That matters more than any single result.

### Part 4: The Story of the Obstacle

There is an old idea, taught by wise people for thousands of years, that goes like this: **the obstacle is not in the way — the obstacle is the way.**

What does that mean? It means that the hard thing you are facing is not stopping you from growing. The hard thing IS how you grow. Without the obstacle, there would be no growth.

Think about your muscles. How do muscles get stronger? By lifting something heavy. The weight is hard. It hurts. Your arms shake. But that difficulty — that exact difficulty — is what makes the muscle grow. If you only lifted easy things, your muscles would never change.

Your mind works the same way. Every time you face a hard problem and work through it, your ability to face hard problems gets stronger. Every time you fail and try again, your ability to recover gets stronger. Every time something does not go your way and you keep going anyway, your ability to persist gets stronger.

The people who grow the most are not the people who have the easiest lives. They are the people who face hard things and do not stop. The difficulty is the training.

### Part 5: People Who Kept Going

History is full of people who could have stopped — and the world would be different if they had.

**Abraham Lincoln** lost elections, failed in business, and experienced deep personal sadness for most of his adult life. He could have stopped trying at any point. He did not. He became one of the most important leaders in American history — not despite the failures, but because the failures taught him things that success never could have.

**Harriet Tubman** escaped slavery and then went back — not once, but many times — to lead others to freedom. Every trip was dangerous. Every trip could have been her last. She did not stop because the task was hard. She kept going because the meaning of what she was doing was bigger than the difficulty.

**The Wright Brothers** built and crashed flying machines for years. People laughed at them. Their machines broke. They rebuilt. They adjusted. They tried again. Each crash taught them something about lift, control, and balance that they could not have learned any other way. The crashes were not failures — they were lessons that eventually produced flight.

These are not fairy tales. These are real people who faced real difficulty and made a choice: the difficulty will teach me something, and I will keep going. That choice is available to every person, every day, including you.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: The "What Did You Learn?" Conversation [After Mistakes — Ongoing]
Every time something goes wrong — a failed attempt, a mistake, a plan that did not work — the first response is one question: "What did you learn from that?" Not "Why did you do that?" Not "What went wrong?" The question is forward-looking: what information did this give you? This is practiced until it becomes the child's own automatic question. Over time, the child should begin asking themselves "What did I learn?" without the adult prompting it.

### Practice 2: Try Again Practice [Daily, As Opportunities Arise]
When the child fails at something and wants to stop, the expectation is: try again. Not immediately if emotions are high — a pause is fine. But the standard is re-engagement, not retreat. The child who falls off the bike gets back on. The child who gets the math problem wrong tries it again with what they learned. The child who drops the ball picks it up and throws again. The practice is the return, not the success.

### Practice 3: Stories of Perseverance [Weekly — Read Aloud]
At least once a week, read aloud a story of someone who persevered through failure. Real people, real difficulty. After the story, discuss:
- What went wrong for this person?
- How did they think about what went wrong?
- What did they do next?
- What would have happened if they had stopped?
This builds a mental library of models — people the child can remember when their own difficulty feels overwhelming.

### Practice 4: Effort Recognition [Daily — Ongoing]
Recognize and name effort, especially when the outcome was not what the child wanted. "You worked really hard on that, and it didn't turn out the way you hoped. The work you put in was real, and you're better at this than you were before you started." This is not consolation. It is accurate observation. The child learns that effort has value independent of outcome.

### Practice 5: The Reframe Game [2–3 Times Per Week]
Take a situation that went wrong — real or hypothetical — and practice seeing it from a different angle. "The tower fell down. That's one way to see it. What's another way? What did the falling tower teach us?" This can be playful. The goal is to develop the mental habit of looking for the second story — the one that turns the event into information rather than defeat.

### Practice 6: The Hard Thing I Did This Week [Weekly — With Mentor]
At the end of each week, the child names one hard thing they faced and what they did about it. Not whether it worked out — what they did. The act of identifying difficulty and naming your response to it is the earliest form of the reflective capacity that IC3.3 develops fully in later tiers.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Your Language Is the Framework

The single most impactful thing you can do for IC3.3 development is to change the language that surrounds failure in your home. This is not about being falsely positive or pretending that things are fine when they are not. It is about consistently orienting language toward learning and forward action rather than toward blame, shame, or defeat.

**Language that builds IC3.3:**
- "What did that teach you?"
- "What would you do differently next time?"
- "You worked hard on that. What did you learn?"
- "That didn't work. What's another way?"
- "Mistakes are how we learn what doesn't work."
- "The effort was real even though the result wasn't what you wanted."

**Language that undermines IC3.3:**
- "Why did you do that?" (implies the child should have known better; orients toward blame)
- "Be more careful!" (implies the mistake was a character failure, not a learning event)
- "That's okay, it doesn't matter." (dismisses the event; the child knows it mattered to them)
- "You're so smart, you shouldn't have gotten that wrong." (ties identity to outcome)
- "Don't worry about it." (signals that difficulty should be avoided or minimized rather than processed)

The shift is subtle but profound. Over hundreds of repetitions, the child absorbs the pattern: when something goes wrong, we learn from it and move forward. That pattern becomes their internal response to difficulty for the rest of their life.

### Modeling Your Own Reframing

Children learn more from what they observe than from what they are told. When you encounter a setback — the recipe fails, the project breaks, you forget something important — narrate your reframing out loud:

- "Well, that didn't go the way I planned. Let me think about what happened."
- "I made a mistake on this. What I'm going to do differently next time is ___."
- "I'm frustrated. But the frustration doesn't help me fix it. Let me figure out what to do next."

You do not need to be perfect at this. In fact, the child seeing you struggle to reframe and then managing it anyway is more instructive than flawless modeling. The honest process — "I wanted to be upset about that, but what I really need to do is figure out the next step" — teaches the child that reframing is a choice you make, not a personality trait you are born with.

### The Difference Between Dismissal and Reframing

This distinction is critical and commonly confused.

**Dismissal** tells the child the difficulty does not matter: "It's fine. Don't worry about it. It's no big deal."

**Reframing** acknowledges the difficulty and then asks what can be made of it: "That was hard. You're disappointed. I get it. Now — what did you learn? What are you going to do next?"

Dismissal teaches the child to suppress their experience. Reframing teaches the child to process their experience and use it. The first produces a person who avoids thinking about hard things. The second produces a person who can face hard things and extract value from them.

Always acknowledge the emotion first. The child who feels dismissed will not be able to hear the reframe. "I can see you're really frustrated that the tower fell. That makes sense — you worked hard on it." Then, after the acknowledgment has landed: "What do you think made it fall? What could you try differently?"
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. The "Try Again" Response

**Developing well:** After a failure — a missed shot, a wrong answer, a collapsed structure — the child's default trend is toward re-engagement. They may be frustrated. They may need a moment. But the trajectory is toward trying again, not toward quitting. Over time, the pause between failure and re-attempt shortens.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently stops after a first failure. "I can't do it" is the automatic response. They do not re-engage without significant adult encouragement or assistance.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the task appropriately challenging? (Too hard = genuine inability, not a reframing problem.) Is the child getting consistent "try again" messaging from all adults in their life, or is one adult rescuing while another expects persistence? Are you modeling the try-again response in your own failures?

### 2. Mistakes as Information

**Developing well:** When asked "What did you learn from that?", the child can articulate something — even if it is simple. "I learned the base needs to be bigger." "I learned I need to read the problem more carefully." They are beginning to see mistakes as data points rather than verdicts.

**Developing slowly:** The child cannot extract any learning from a mistake. The response is either emotional ("I hate this") or blank ("I don't know"). There is no connection between what went wrong and what to do differently.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you asking the question consistently? (The habit requires repetition.) Are you accepting simple answers and building from there? (A 5-year-old's answer will be basic — that is fine.) Are you demonstrating the connection between your own mistakes and what you learned from them?

### 3. Separating Effort from Outcome

**Developing well:** The child can work hard, not get the result they wanted, and still recognize the value of the effort. They may be disappointed in the outcome but do not conclude that the effort was meaningless. "I tried really hard and I didn't win, but I got faster."

**Developing slowly:** The child equates failed outcome with wasted effort. "I practiced and I still lost, so practicing doesn't matter." Effort feels pointless unless it produces the desired result.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you recognizing effort independent of outcome? (If praise and recognition only come when the child succeeds, the lesson is clear: only outcomes matter.) Are you pointing out the gains that happened regardless of the result? "You didn't win, but look at your time — it's twenty seconds faster than last month."

### 4. Engagement with Stories of Perseverance

**Developing well:** The child is engaged during read-aloud stories of perseverance. They can retell the story, identify what went wrong for the character, and explain what the character did about it. They may reference these stories when facing their own difficulty.

**Developing slowly:** The child is disengaged during these stories or cannot connect them to their own experience. The stories remain abstract rather than formational.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the stories age-appropriate and genuinely engaging? (A story that bores the child teaches nothing.) Are you discussing the stories with genuine interest, not as a lesson to be extracted? Are you choosing stories with real difficulty and real humanity, not sanitized triumph narratives?

### 5. Early Self-Reframing

**Developing well:** The child begins to reframe on their own, without adult prompting. After a setback, you hear them say something like, "That's okay, I'll try it a different way," or "That one didn't work, but now I know ___." This is the internalization of the framework.

**Developing slowly:** Reframing only happens when the adult initiates it. The child does not spontaneously generate a constructive interpretation of setbacks.

**Note:** Spontaneous self-reframing is the most advanced marker at this tier. A child who is not yet doing this but is responding well to adult-initiated reframing is on track. Do not rush this. The internalization takes time.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Forced Positivity
"Look on the bright side!" is not reframing. It is dismissal dressed up as encouragement. The child whose genuine frustration is met with relentless positivity learns that their feelings are unwelcome, not that difficulty can be processed. Reframing starts with honest acknowledgment of the difficulty. The child must feel that the adult takes their experience seriously before they can engage with a different interpretation of it.

### Praising Talent Instead of Effort
Carol Dweck's research is unambiguous on this point: children who are praised for being "smart" or "talented" develop a fixed mindset — they avoid challenges because failure would threaten their identity as a smart person. Children who are praised for effort, strategy, and persistence develop a growth mindset — they seek challenges because difficulty is where growth happens. Say "You worked really hard on that" rather than "You're so smart." Say "That was a clever strategy" rather than "You're a natural."

### Rescuing from Difficulty
The parent who steps in at the first sign of struggle is removing the very experience IC3.3 depends on. The child who never experiences failure cannot develop a relationship with failure. Allow age-appropriate struggle. Be present, be calm, but do not remove the difficulty. The child who works through a hard problem with you watching calmly from nearby learns something no amount of instruction can teach: "I can handle hard things."

### Inconsistent Messaging
If one parent or mentor models reframing while another responds to difficulty with frustration, blame, or rescue, the child receives contradictory signals. IC3.3 formation requires consistent language across all adult relationships in the child's life. Align with your co-parent or fellow mentors on the language and approach before expecting the child to internalize it.

### Over-Processing
Not every mistake requires a learning conversation. Sometimes the tower falls and the child rebuilds it without comment, and that is the right response — the reframing happened internally and naturally. If you turn every small setback into a Socratic dialogue, the child will start to dread making mistakes because of the conversation that follows. Use the "What did you learn?" question selectively and genuinely. Watch for moments where it will land, and leave the rest alone.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Something That Didn't Work the First Time
**Strong:** Recalls a specific event. Describes what happened, what they did, and what was different on the second attempt. Shows awareness that the first failure informed the second attempt.
**Weak:** Cannot recall an example, or describes the failure but not the retry. "I messed up and it was bad."

### Prompt 2: What Is a Mistake?
**Strong:** "A mistake is when something doesn't work out. It teaches you what not to do." The child understands mistakes as information, not as identity.
**Weak:** "A mistake is when you're bad at something." The child equates mistakes with personal failure.

### Prompt 3: Character Story
**Strong:** Names a specific person and specific difficulty. Can explain why they kept going and what would have been lost if they stopped. Shows engagement with the narrative.
**Weak:** Vague recollection. "Someone was brave and didn't give up." No specifics.

### Prompt 4: Effort Without Desired Outcome
**Strong:** Can describe working hard at something that did not turn out as hoped. Acknowledges the disappointment but also names what was gained — skill, knowledge, experience. "I didn't win but I got better."
**Weak:** "I tried hard and it didn't work, so it was a waste." Effort and outcome are fused.

### Prompt 5: Control vs. No Control
**Strong:** Can give examples of what they control (effort, attitude, trying again) and what they do not control (weather, other people's choices, outcomes). The distinction is emerging.
**Weak:** Everything feels like their fault or nothing feels within their influence. No distinction between the two categories.

### Prompt 6: Hardest Thing This Week
**Strong:** Names a specific difficulty and describes their response. Shows honest self-awareness. "I was really frustrated when ___ but I ___."
**Weak:** "Nothing was hard." (Either dishonest or not yet self-aware enough.)
